key_facts: Attacks the Nov. 2025 Discharge Review itself — ten documented facial defects: (a) Case Type contradiction (PC 3000(b)(5)(B) vs '10-year term'); (b) fabricated 6.5-year minimum from academic research (Cortoni et al. 2010), not statute; (c) fabricated Policy 19-03 exclusion; (d) 'Eagan' copy-paste error — Noguchi's review names a different parolee, proving no individualized review; (e) contradictory treatment characterizations; (f) scoring without aggregation (14 pts = 'Increase' band, yet 'Retain'); (g) violations from unpromulgated GPS protocol (underground regulation); (h) failure to weigh mitigating evidence (LS/CMI 5, Noguchi's own commendation); (i) no CTM, Form 3043, or good-cause findings; (j) report never served (notice field blank). The Discharge Review Report (CDCR Form 1502-DR) received final approval on November 19, 2025; the first date of the ongoing unlawful parole supervision is December 1, 2025. The incident location is 612 Carlton Avenue, Stockton, California 95203. Plaintiff/claimant Brendan Ngehsi Newanforbi (CDCR #) resides at , Stockton, CA 95204. As a result of the defective report, claimant has been subjected to ongoing unlawful parole supervision including: 24/7 GPS monitoring, curfew, travel and association restrictions, warrantless searches, and mandatory HOPE  treatment multiple times per week — all without lawful authority. Being labeled 'non-compliant' or 'failed treatment' under HOPE is structurally impossible under that program's own blanket policy, making such designations in the official record arbitrary and tortious. Out-of-pocket compliance costs include transportation to HOPE treatment (approximately 30 miles round-trip per session, multiple times per week at IRS standard mileage rate), 6–8 hours per week time value, program fees, polygraph examinations, mandated assessments, and GPS equipment maintenance and charging costs. Individual defendants Moua, Noguchi, Dodd, and Rojo acted willfully and oppressively with conscious disregard of judicially condemned practices established in Dwyer (2023) and in light of April 2025 regulatory amendments to 15 CCR. A parallel federal §1983 action arises from the November 1, 2024 CHP stop on I-580 East in Livermore (Officers Santiago Meza-Gonzalez #023295 and Melano #023054) that triggered the parole violation hold underlying this matter. W-2 documented earnings from 6 carriers: $39,553 (2022), $11,145 (2023), $55,894 (2024). But-for capacity: $72,000/year. Documented lost wages: $109,408. Loss of liberty valued at a minimum of $100/day since November 19, 2025 (final approval date); approximately 84 days of accrued liberty damages through February 10, 2026 (claim presentation cutoff) = $8,400 at time of filing, continuing to accrue daily.
procedural_status: Government Claim filed with California DGS/ORIM under Gov. Code § 910 on March 21, 2026 (claimant's electronic signature and declaration date); incident date (first date of ongoing unlawful parole supervision) is December 1, 2025; CDCR Form 1502-DR final approval date November 19, 2025. Gov. claim deemed rejected 03/27/2026 (§912.4(c)). Complaint for Damages filed 05/29/2026 in San Joaquin Superior Court. Jury trial demanded. Filed within 6-month suit deadline (Gov. Code §945.6(a)(1)). Unlimited civil jurisdiction (damages exceed $25,000, continuing to accrue daily). No statutory cap on general or punitive damages.
evidence: Form 1502-DR (10 defects) — Discharge_Review_Report.pdf (attached to Gov. Claim)
Verified Complaint for Damages (Exhibit A to Gov. Claim) — prayer for relief on pp. 21–22 with per-diem minimums expressly pled
Dwyer v. CDCR court orders (2023) — condemning same/similar CDCR practices
ACSOL v. Macomber ruling — persuasive/binding authority on CDCR parole discharge obligations
April 2025 regulatory amendments to 15 CCR — post-Dwyer changes consciously disregarded by individual defendants
HOPE  treatment program records and policies — establish 'non-compliant'/'failed treatment' designations are structurally impossible under program's blanket policy
GPS monitoring records — evidence of 24/7 custodial restraint imposed without lawful authority
Polygraph examination records and mandated assessment records — out-of-pocket compliance cost evidence
IRS standard mileage rate calculations — transportation costs to HOPE treatment sessions
California Penal Code § 3001(a)(2) and 15 CCR § 3574 — mandatory discharge statutes
CHP arrest report and CHP correspondence characterizing I-580 encounter as 'detention' (Nov. 1, 2024 incident underlying parole hold)
Administrative chemical refusal designation — triggered CDL suspension until February 2026
damages: $250,000 - $600,000+ (continuing to accrue). Liberty loss component: minimum $100/day from Nov. 19, 2025 (84 days through Feb. 10, 2026 = $8,400 at claim filing, accruing daily). Lost wages: $109,408 documented (but-for capacity $72,000/yr). Out-of-pocket compliance costs: HOPE transportation (~30 mi/round-trip, multiple sessions/week at IRS rate), program fees, polygraph and assessment fees, GPS maintenance. Punitive damages sought against Moua, Noguchi, Dodd, and Rojo individually. No statutory cap on general or punitive damages. Total claimed exceeds $25,000 (unlimited civil jurisdiction).
risk: §820.2 discretionary immunity — rebutted by mandatory/ministerial duties under PC §3001(a)(2) and §3574 (Johnson v. State, Sullivan v. County of LA). 'Eagan' copy-paste error inserting another parolee's name into Plaintiff's official Discharge Review Report justification is uniquely powerful proof of no individualized review and facial procedural deficiency. Defendants also fabricated eligibility rules (nonexistent '6.5-year' minimum and '10-year parole term' exclusion) with no basis in California law or regulation, constituting ultra vires conduct that negates discretionary immunity. Individual defendants consciously disregarded Dwyer (2023) judicial findings and April 2025 regulatory amendments to 15 CCR, supporting punitive damages. Continuing tort: each day of unlawful parole supervision = separate act, tolling accrual. 'Non-compliant'/'failed treatment' HOPE designations are structurally impossible under that program's own blanket policy, adding arbitrary-and-capricious/tortious label claim. No statutory cap on general or punitive damages.
defendants: Long Moua (#7472); Gary Noguchi (#7284); Keely Dodd (#6462); Andrew Derrick (#6910); Joseelyn Rojo; CDCR; DOES 1-20. Punitive damages sought individually against Moua, Noguchi, Dodd, and Rojo for willful and oppressive conduct.

documents.label: Complaint — Newanforbi v. Dodd et al.
